sentences.text: We moved out from the forest because when the Russian came, they were thinking that we are [GERMAN].
You know what is it, [GERMAN]?
Spy.
So, took us about a couple of hours to convince them.
Then come in a commander, and he is Jewish!
From the Russian.
He said, are you are Jew?
Yeah!
We are Jewish partisan.
That's what I wanted to see.
And this was our [RUSSIAN].
This was ours.
And they were very-- [RUSSIAN] meaning salvation?
Salvation.
And he was very good to us.
And he said, OK, you don't have to stay now in the forest, because they were fighting all over the German.
The German were running away in the forest, like [POLISH]-- like mice.
So we went away to the city.
The German already left the city.
Did people just disperse, or--
Yes.
No, we leave together.
From the forest we leave together.
This was nothing decided.
This was the instinct.
There was nothing in-- nothing-- nobody to talk.
The Russian army was on the front.
They were running after this.
Nobody to talk to.
So you have to decide it-- we cannot stay in the forest.
We have to go to the city, from where we are-- from where we went away.
So other one said, we are going to Lida.
I am from Lida.
I said I'm going to Novogrudok.
I am from Novogrudok.
Maybe I will find somebody-- something, something.
And everybody went away in his place.
But-- Did you know what had happened in the outside world?
We knew.
We knew-- first of all, we knew that they killed all of them-- all the Jews.
We knew.
Because the forest people-- the forest-- the villages, the people from the villages, they were telling us that they saw two big trucks with people killed-- Jewish people, with children.
Like Babi Yar, and the other things.
Children, live children, they were throwing in the grounds.
The people were telling us everything.
We knew.
We knew!
Yes.
We knew.
We knew everything.
But we couldn't-- we didn't-- you see, we knew, but you couldn't think about it clear.
When we came to the city, then this came crashing on us.
No, right away.
We knew right away when we came to the city.
The first--
We knew that they're killing the Jews.
I knew.
I had a Moskva-- I had a Moskva-- a cousin a doctor, and a cousin a professor.
They caught my brother, Itzchak.
One brother had papers by plane to go to Israel.
And he went. by [NON-ENGLISH].
The other one, he had a girl, and he had to wait for her.
And was no more planes.
So the Russian caught him to the army.
When he was in the army, he was [NON-ENGLISH]-- he was-- Wounded.
Wounded.
And they took him to Moskva-- Moskva hospital.
When he waked up in Moskva hospital, he said he doesn't remember the name, but he knows that he has aunt one, and the other one.
And the name Nochimowski-- because my mother was a Nochiowski.
This is my mother's family.
And he's sure that they will know, if he will tell them Boldo is here, they will know.
And how do you like that?
It's the only-- there was a gazeta-- was a-- a paper on the wall that-- one Boldo, he's-- was on the front, and he was-- they brought him here to the hospital.
And he was looking for two ladies, one a professor, one is a doctor, Nochimowski.
And you can see him here, you see.
In 20 minutes, they find out, and they came to see my brother.
So then, he said that he has already letters from me, that my brother Yankele was killed when he took it off my mother.
And he knew that my brother-- two brothers-- are in Israel, but they didn't know that the [NON-ENGLISH] was dead already.
So he asked them to write a letter to Israel that he's alive.
And he still will fight to take [YIDDISH] for his brother.
Revenge?
Yeah.
And the last day from the fighting in Prussia-- Prussia [NON-ENGLISH]-- he died.
The letter was the last day before the fight.
He said, all the time he went to the fight with such a good heart.
He knew that he will live.
But this time, I don't know, he said, he has something-- a bad feeling.
In Prussia.
Prussia [NON-ENGLISH].
That's between [NON-ENGLISH] and Gdansk.
Prussia.
Prussia is on the Baltic.
Yes.
And he said he has a bad feeling that he will not go out from that.
So he said to me, if you will not have two, three days' letters from me, remember that I am, too-- I went to my brother.
And this is-- after a week, my sister-in-law Lily got a telephone, and a telegram, that they were looking for Sonia Bielski-- Sonia Boldo.
They came to Lily-- to Lily-- to his wife-- and they gave it to her that my brother is a hero, and he fall in Prussia, this-and-this day.
This was the last day.
They were divided.
The women and the children what that didn't-- couldn't go with number 11, they had horses and wagons.
And they put them on the wagons, and they went to Novogrudok.
And that was not far.
Remember one thing-- that's not a car, what you're going 10, 15 miles in nothing.
But this, by foot, took them a day.
And they camped in Novogrudok.
Novogrudok-- this was probably about 12 kilometer.
What is it-- nothing or nothing.
But when you're going by foot, it's something else from the forest.
I don't remember the date that I left.
Yeah-- the month-- we know when we left.
We left in-- this was in June-- in June-- in June you left the forest.
And it didn't have where to stay, so the command chief said, go wherever you wanted.
Go-- the war is finished!
But they didn't give them times.
Because, right away, the Russian-- this was already the Russian command chief-- asked for the people young what they can fight at the front.
And they took away all the young people.
And 50% of them died.
They was not prepared to-- to-- to- an army, to go fight.
There were fighters in the partisan on the streets.
But to fight, 50% of them died-- yes.
Oh, sure, I know.
Sure, I know.
Yeah.
No-- I knew-- I knew a couple of them.
But who-- what is it.
They didn't have no parents already-- nobody.
They were fighting the partisan a couple of years.
And they fell there.
They took them right away.
They didn't give them a chance.
Which ones?
Oh, the Bielski brothers-- something else, the Bielski brothers.
They took away Asael.
They took away Asael to the army.
The Russian.
Took away Asael to the army.
And the wife couldn't go away with him to-- to sit in place for two, three weeks.
They will forget about him.
He said that he cannot go to put himself away from them.
If they call him into the army, he has to go.
Asael.
And he died.
And she was pregnant, and she had the baby.
After a couple of days, she had the baby.
The baby is the name of her father, Asael-- Assaela.
And they live in Israel, where she's writing now a book about this father.
Very intelligent girl.
I took her out from Israel here.
And she was studying here.
She finished college here.
And she's an English major, and she's writing.
She's a very intelligent girl.
And that's it.
And Chaja.
Yes.
Yeah, yeah.
They didn't fight.
No.
No.
No, no.
Did not obey.
Tuvia went already someplace, Zus went someplace, Asael went in another place.
Me and Zusia went-- my mother and father wanted to go to Novogrudok, to see the house-- this.
And Zusia wants to go to Lida.
So I said to mother, you go to Novogrudok, and I will go to Lida.
We'll see where is better, so come to each other.
And that's what the story.
I went to Lida.
And Zusia got a big promotion there, from the [NON-ENGLISH]-- from the-- from the-- this, uh-- what is it called?
The-- from the-- white-- from the Russian.
And they didn't take him to the army, because of that, that they gave to him a big thing.
He was working for them.
Yes?
For the Russian.
Tuvia was working, too, for the Russian, but he did something what they didn't like it.
So they wanted to arrest him.
What did he do?
He did something.
I don't know.
I was not with him.
They wanted to arrest him, so we decided if he will arrest him, so probably this will be [INAUDIBLE] together, this was already this.
So we-- and Asael was already in the army, and he fell already.
Yeah, the war was over-- sure.
I was in Lida.
In Lida.
About four, five months.
And everything was OK till something was wrong.
So, anyway, we run away from there.
We run away to Lublin-- to Wilno.
Oh, we took a room by an old lady.
And we lived there.
For now.
We didn't know where to go, what to do, yet-- nothing.
But Zusia was working.
And I was working like an accountant-- not accountant-- a bookkeeper.
You see?
So we made already a living, me and him.
And my father and mother was in Novogrudok, and they were sending me every day more money and more bread and more [INAUDIBLE] and everything.
Where were your mother and father living?
In Novogrudok.
Novogrudok.
She went away, and the Polish what she was living in our house gave to them two rooms in our house.
You understand?
So they had already a place.
And my father, he's very-- began already to do something, and he had everything like that.
So he was sending us the food.
In Lida?
Yes.
Yes.
And we were working.
I am telling you, we were working.
Zusia was working, and I was working, and we were very happy-- quite happy.
I was very happy.
I was very happy with Zusia.
I loved him very much.
And he was terrific-- very good to my parents.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Run away to Wilno.
And the nights were terrible.
This was December, I think-- whatever.
It was so cold-- terrible cold.
We stayed in Wilno a couple of days, by friends of ours, two-- but they went away before us, to Wilno, And they gave us a room.
But we stay only about four days.
They were telling us they're looking for the Bielskis in Wilno.
They knew that we ran away, you understand?
And they're looking for us.

So, right away, we left with the first [NON-ENGLISH]-- the first-- [NON-ENGLISH] not-- train-- what the-- in the wintertime, the oil and everything is going with that-- you know, for the-- for the heat, what they bring in from Poland-- in Poland.
Oil?
Heating oil?
[POLISH].
Coal.
And me and Lilke, and Tuvia, and Zus, were sitting on the coal, like that.
Nobody should see us.
And, like that, we went to Lublin.
In Lublin, we came in Lublin already, somebody from the Aliyah Bet was waiting for us.
And he said, you are the Bielskis?
Yes.
I have something for the Bielskis.
Israel was not Israel, it was Palestine.
This was the [NON-ENGLISH].
And he send you a letter that you have to come to Romania.
In Romania will wait for you four certificates.
And you have to go to Israel.
But till the certificate came, took about six months.
So we stayed in Romania, in [NON-ENGLISH].
How did we go?
The number 11-- everything with number 11.
We took a truck, we took a-- we took the-- whatever it took.
We crossed as Persian Jew-- not Persian-- Greek Jew.
Now, this was so many people, they were going from one place to the other one, we didn't have a place where to sit.
So my husband tore his coat-- he was always smart-- tore his coat, and he begin to [GERMAN].
You-- you understand what I'm talking to you?
Lice.
So everybody was running away from him!
With their running away, we had four places.
We find the four places, and we're sitting.
It was already warm.
And we came like that to Hungary.
And we throw out the things that-- the-- everything.
And we took other things.
And from Hungary, we went-- Czechoslovakia, Hungary, and Romania.
And Romania was six months sitting.
We was in [NON-ENGLISH], this was like a country place, near-- near Bucharest.
Until they said to us, that's enough, you're coming, and you're going to Israel.
No.
No--
Of us?
The Aliyah-- the Bet.
People from Israel, they were taking care of us.
Yes, they gave us food, and we had a couple dollars-- the-- the red-- the money-- the red-- what my father and mother gave to us.
You know, the Russian money.
Yeah, coin-- we changed, and we ate.
We ate good.
And then we went to Romania and, with the we went to Israel.
Ship.
And we came to Israel.
This was October 1945.
It was Saturday.
We came to the-- to Israel.
Haifa.
We were all crying.
First of all, I didn't know that I lost a brother.
All of them, they knew.

Because when I lost my brother, the [NON-ENGLISH], the whole papers what they come in to the Israelis what were working there in Europe, there came that Zalman Boldo [INAUDIBLE] got killed, this-and-this day.
Two weeks before I came to Israel, he got killed.
He was a Palmachnik, one of the best Palmachnikas.
And a friendly fire shot him.
OK.
This is it.
We were waiting about four, five hours.
And I-- I see nobody from my brothers is here.
They didn't see me, the war-- nothing.
It's impossible they should not see me.
After four hours, I saw my brother, the oldest-- Motele, came in.
And waving to me.
I said, OK, I see my brother.
And when he came on the-- on, yeah, they let him in on the-- on the ship, I said, tell me only the truth.
I don't want to-- I don't want to go down.
I have such a bad feelings, I said.
Tell me, what is with Itzchak-- I mean Zalman.
He said yes, mein kind, I didn't-- I couldn't-- I couldn't come.
I was four hours waiting till I-- I was OK to come to you.
No-- I came straight--
Israel, yes.
And he knew, because the people that-- from the Israeli [NON-ENGLISH], they told him that we are coming.
You understand?
They told him.
And he was a very big shishke.
He was, too, a Palmachnik.
He was working.
He was a captain in the Palmach.
He was always working for the [NON-ENGLISH].
So he said this is it.
So I said, tell me, how has it happened?
What is it happened?
And he told me.
This is the story.
This is.
And down from the ship, we went right away to Tel Aviv.
From Tel Aviv, we went to Kibbutz Dan.
He was in Kibbutz Dan.
Lilke went, Tuvia and me and Zus.
We were in Kibbutz Dan about four days.
And the Syrian began their to shooting.
This was terrible, and I was-- I was all shaking up.
As Zus said, I'm sorry, I cannot stay with her here.
I have to take her to Tel Aviv, because she will fail-- she will be sick.
And that's what happened.
We went right away to Tel Aviv.
And there was one working [NON-ENGLISH] in the Szochnut, Cohen, in the Szochnut.
Cohen.
Cohen.
He was working in the [NON-ENGLISH], the Szorchnut.
And he said, Boldo's daughter?
I am taking her to my house.
And he took me and Zusia to his house, gave us a room, and feed us, tille we got-- till we got a [NON-ENGLISH], apartment from the [NON-ENGLISH].
About Bielskis?
Oh, sure, we came in right away.
They were writing about us, and they wrote the first book, [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]?
The people of the forest.
And this was written there.
And the whole story.
He was sitting, writing, and they was telling, day by day, how that happened.
Yes.
Was special treatment for us.
They gave us right away [NON-ENGLISH]-- two apartments.
They give us later to make a living.
OK, a kitchen and a room.
That's right.
They give us later-- to make a living, they give us medallions, taxi medallions, that were very expensive.
They gave us without a penny.
And we made right away a living.
My husband never was waiting to hand him something.
He took everything by himself.
He was working very hard, from the first day what he opened his eyes.
In Poland, he was working very hard for the family.
In the mill, in Poland.
And then he married the-- the lady-- he was working to her.
And then, when he lost it, the partisan.
And after the partisan, when we came to Israel, he was working very hard, too.
And he came to something.
I had right away apartment in Ramat Gan.
My children went to--
My first son was born-- in-- my first son, Jay, was born in-- I came in Israel in 1945.
My--
Yeah, when I was pregnant-- and six months-- six months-- five months-- I wrote to my mother in Paris-- she was in Paris, then, by the family-- that I am pregnant.
She asked me, listen, I know you love him and he loves you.
But I would like for the children you should be married now.
So we went to a rabbi, and he married me off, me and Zus.
When we came here, to the United States, he said-- the justice-- he send out my son-- my oldest, Jay-- does your son knows that you marry-- you marry your husband when you were pregnant?
I said, yes.
This was a war.
Yeah, this is it.
So Jay was born in 1963.
David was born in-- David was-- no, [NON-ENGLISH]-- Jay was born in 1969-- Jay.
David was born in-- something wrong with me.
Jay's birthday is December 9, and we were in Israel in '45, and he was born in '49.
David was born in '50-- '51.
And Zvika was born in '53.
All of them, they're born in Israel-- in Assuta.
I decided I couldn't take the Arabs.
Whatever you're closing a window, what-- I was thinking that the Arabs are coming to knock me in the window.
This was my-- my-- I was afraid of the children, of the boys.
I had so much afraid of that.
I had-- I felt it-- the knocking from the German, from everybody.
And I said, I cannot take it.
What will be when the children will go to the army?
So my brother, my kibbutzim said, Sonia, I don't care.
You can go to the United States, but don't take away your children from here.
But my children went anyway to the army there.
When did you come here?
Hmm?
July 7, '57.
Here?
He bought a gas station.
He was always in cars.
He bought a gas station.
And he was very, very, hard-working, and he made a living from the gas station.
We built trucking.
We had 10 trucks-- big ones.
And, from the trucking, he built a fleet of taxis.
And I had everything-- the best of everything.
My parents?
They were in Paris.
And they wanted to go to Israel, to see me, so they came to Israel.
And the war began there-- yeah?
In '48 began the war of independence.
Yes?
So my mother couldn't take this, too.
I said, why should you stay here?
Go away to the United States-- you have there a family.
And they had a family.
They had two sisters and two brothers.
So she went away here.
Later, I came from Israel here.
And she said, you know, mein kind?
I was already here enough.
I want to go to Israel.
So my husband went to Israel and bought them an apartment, in Ramat Gan.
And he bought them all the things what with the apartment is going-- deluxe things and everything.
And put them to the apartment, and they lived.
My mother lived a year longer than my father.
My daddy was probably 98, and my mother was 99.
But my father was-- to the last day, he was with his head all right.
My mother-- two years she didn't know what is with her.
We kept for her a woman-- two woman.
But nobody could stay already with her.
So I was with my father three months, who never told me not to stay.
I went home with my father was OK.
My mother-- no, my father died from a heart attack.
Yeah?
After three months, my mother died.
I was with my mother three months.
How old were your sons, when you came to the United States?
Mine oldest was 10-- 10, David was seven, and Zvikela was four.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
In all the ways.
In all the ways.
First of all, me and my husband was like one head and one hand.
We say like that-- I don't want it nothing.
I don't want something what I cannot reach.
But I want only to give the children education like my mother and father gave.
